steps.prompt: Which word is a verb form (finite)?

hints.Ductus: 'Ductus' is a participle (verbal adjective), not the finite verb here

hints.to eat: 'to eat' is 'edere/esse' — but 'est' here is from 'esse' (to be)

reveal.gloss: he/it is (used with participle = was led)

hints.perfect: Perfect of 'esse' would be 'fuit' — 'est' is present, forming a periphrastic perfect passive
hints.imperfect: Imperfect of 'esse' would be 'erat' — 'est' is present tense
hints.future: Future of 'esse' would be 'erit' — 'est' is present tense

hints.edo, edere, edi, esum: These are principal parts of 'edo' (to eat)
hints.eo, ire, ivi, itum: These are principal parts of 'eo' (to go)
hints.do, dare, dedi, datum: These are principal parts of 'do' (to give)

steps.prompt: 'Ductus' is a participle. What is the infinitive of the verb it comes from?

hints.dico, dicere, dixi, dictum: These are principal parts of 'dico' (to say)
hints.doceo, docere, docui, doctum: These are principal parts of 'doceo' (to teach)
hints.disco, discere, didici, —: These are principal parts of 'disco' (to learn)

hints.nominative: Nominative would be the subject, but 'desertum' follows 'in' expressing motion
hints.ablative: Ablative with 'in' would mean 'in/at'; here 'in' + accusative means 'into'

hints.1st declension: 1st declension nouns have genitive in -ae — 'deserti' ends in -i
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension nouns have genitive in -is — 'deserti' ends in -i
hints.4th declension: 4th declension nouns have genitive in -us — 'deserti' ends in -i

translation: He was led into the desert
